1:
The grammatical-historical commentators fix the time and the place. Charles Ellicott reads the opening literally — "at the return of the year," as the margin has it — and refers it back to the close of the previous chapter: Joab had wintered at Jerusalem and now returned to Ammon, while David, having hurried his own campaign against Hadarezer, sent the bulk of the army to join him. Ellicott notes the curious textual point that the Hebrew as pointed reads "when messengers go forth," corrected in the margin to "when kings go forth," and glosses "destroyed the children of Ammon" by 1 Chron. 20:1, which speaks rather of wasting the country — foraging parties laying it waste while Rabbah was besieged. Adam Clarke and John Wesley agree on the season: it was spring, the time "when the ground is fit for the march of soldiers, and brings forth provision for man and beast"; Clarke, following Calmet, wonders whether two campaigns were fought, one in autumn and one in spring. John Gill dates the turn of the year to the month Adar.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the verse as the first stone in David's fall. The war is David's glory — pursuing the Ammonites, avenging the wrong done his ambassadors — yet its beauty is "stained and sullied by sin," and the fatal note is the last clause: David "tarried still at Jerusalem." To the Syrian war he had gone in person (2 Sam. 10:17); now he devolves the danger upon others and keeps his own ease. Henry's maxim governs the whole tragedy that follows: "When we are out of the way of our duty we are in the way of temptation." The wesleyan-arminian Wesley says the same in a breath — had David "been now in his post, at the head of his forces, he had been out of the way of temptation."

The witnesses scarcely divide; their one small disagreement is Clarke's guess at a single spring campaign or two. What unites them is that the verse is a threshold. It is idleness, not the Ammonites, that undoes the king: the man who should have been in the field is left with an empty afternoon and a palace roof, and adultery is here reckoned an iniquity the very judges must punish (Job 31:11). And they let the shadow of the whole chapter fall across the sentence, for the siege of Rabbah begun here is the frame in which the ruin of Bathsheba and Uriah will unfold.
2:
The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene with care. Charles Ellicott pictures the late afternoon: David rose from the customary Oriental siesta and walked on the palace roof, "the pleasantest part of an eastern house" in the cool of the day; the palace stood high on Zion and looked down on the open courts of the lower city, where in one a beautiful woman was bathing — a spot "probably entirely concealed from every other point of observation than the roof." Keil and Delitzsch mark the gravity of the hour, calling it "a turning-point not only in the inner life of the great king, but also in the history of his reign"; hitherto David had kept free from the grosser sins, and they trace an opening to the tolerated evil of polygamy, which "encouraged licentiousness and the tendency to sensual excesses." John Gill notes it was still light enough to see the object distinctly, and Wesley adds that the washing was "probably from some ceremonial pollution," a bath in her garden.

The wesleyan-arminian voices turn the couch itself into a warning. John Wesley: David "arose from off his bed," and "the bed of sloth often proves the bed of lust." Adam Clarke presses the harder question, and here the witnesses divide. Was the bathing innocent or contrived? Clarke suspects design — "How could any woman of delicacy expose herself where she could be so fully and openly viewed? Did she not know that she was at least in view of the king's terrace? Was there no design in all this?" — yet at once supplies the other side from his travels, where in a Bengal town women bathe openly morning and evening, so that "David might have seen Bath-sheba, and no blame attach to her." Ellicott takes the innocent reading: the court was concealed, and "from which no harm was suspected."

The deeper cause, the grammatical-historical Ellicott urges, lies not in the woman but in the man's long ease. David's fall was "consequent upon his long course of uninterrupted prosperity and power, which had somewhat intoxicated him and thrown him off his guard." He adds the Scripture's own honesty — "It is no part of the plan of Scripture to cover up or excuse the sins of even its greatest heroes" — and closes with the word of the latin-fathers, quoting Augustine, which the whole company would own: David's fall "should put upon their guard those who have not fallen, and save from despair those who have." Where they differ over Bathsheba's intent, they agree on David's: the sin came in at the eye, and the guard that prosperity had lowered let it in.
3:
The grammatical-historical commentators concentrate on identity. David "sent and inquired after the woman," and Keil and Delitzsch with Charles Ellicott establish who she was — Bathsheba, daughter of Eliam, wife of Uriah the Hittite; in Chronicles the names appear as Bath-shua daughter of Ammiel (1 Chron. 3:5), the same names with their parts transposed, "as Scripture names are often varied." Adam Clarke draws out their meaning — Eliam and Ammiel, "the people of my God" and "the God of my people," a covenant name. Ellicott presses the aggravation: Uriah stands in the roll of David's thirty chief heroes (2 Sam. 23:39), "a brave and noble-minded soldier," so that "the knowledge of Bath-sheba's being a married woman, and the wife of one of his chief warriors, does not check him."

The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley read the inquiry itself as the fatal step. Gill allows that the first question may have been innocent — perhaps, if she were unmarried, David meant only to take her to wife as he had taken others — "but it would have been better for him not to have inquired at all," better to have "endeavoured to stifle the motions raised in him at the sight of her." Wesley is blunter: "instead of suppressing that desire which the sight of his eyes had kindled, he seeks rather to feed it," inquiring first who she was.

The witnesses do not contend here; together they mark the moment the sin passed from the eye to the will. The very answer that should have stopped him — she is a wife, and the wife of Uriah — becomes only information to be worked around. Gill's counsel is the pastoral point they land on: the safe course is not to parley with temptation but to turn from it before inquiry gives it a foothold, for once David asks the woman's name he has already resolved not to master the desire but to serve it. The name of Uriah the Hittite, honourably recorded, hangs over the rest of the chapter as a standing witness against the king who first learned it here.
4:
The grammatical-historical commentators narrate the deed without flinching. Keil and Delitzsch: "the information brought to him that the beautiful woman was married was not enough to stifle the sensual desires," and over it they set the apostle's law — "when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin" (James 1:15). All the witnesses weigh what the text does and does not say. Adam Clarke: "we hear nothing of her reluctance, and there is no evidence that she was taken by force." Keil finds in "he took her, and she came to him" no hint that David used "craft or violence, but rather that she came at his request without any hesitation." Charles Ellicott agrees the sending "does not imply the use of violence."

Hence the division over Bathsheba's guilt. Keil holds she "is not to be regarded as free from blame," her open bathing saying "not much for her feminine modesty." Ellicott, citing her later conduct (1 Kings 2:13), calls her "a woman of little discretion," who "yielded to David's will without resistance, perhaps flattered by the approach of the king." Yet all keep the proportion Ellicott states plainly: "David, nevertheless, was a far greater offender." The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the persuasion, not force — David "took her from her own house into his palace, not by force, but by persuasion."

The sharpest commentary, as the reformed Gill and the historians alike observe, is the closing clause — "for she was purified from her uncleanness." Ellicott: under the Law she was unclean until evening, and so remained "scrupulous in this detail while conscious of a capital crime and a high offence against God." Wesley draws the refrain that governs the whole chapter: "See how all the way to sin is down hill! When men begin, they cannot soon stop themselves." The witnesses let the ceremonial nicety stand as the bitterest irony of all — a conscience exact about the ritual and dead to the sin. Keil presses the deeper lesson from the swiftness of the fall: the man who a moment before had asked only the woman's name has now, in a single evening, broken the seventh commandment against the wife of one of his own heroes, and the sin that "she returned unto her house" seems briefly to bury is already carrying its own discovery within it. What the verse narrates so plainly, the witnesses read as the whole shape of temptation — desire indulged becomes a deed that no ceremony can cleanse and no privacy can hide.
5:
The verse is only three words in the woman's mouth — "I am with child" — but the witnesses hear a whole peril in them. The reformed John Gill notes that the conception is the thing "whereby the sin would be discovered, and shame, and disgrace, or worse, would follow upon it." Keil and Delitzsch read the terse message as itself an appeal: Bathsheba "sent word to David," and the sending "involved an appeal to him to take the necessary steps to avert the evil consequences of the sin." Adam Clarke, matter-of-fact, ties the conception back to his note on the time of her purifying.

The grammatical-historical commentators name the "worse." The shadow behind the message is the Law. Keil and Charles Ellicott both cite it: "the law required that both adulterer and adulteress should be put to death" (Leviticus 20:10). Ellicott: "her sin must now become known, and by the Law adulterers must both be punished with death." It is this, the witnesses imply, that turns a private sin into a public emergency and sets David groping for concealment. Keil sees in the very brevity of her message — "she sent and told David, and said, I am with child" — the whole weight of the crisis: no reproach, no plea, only the bare fact that now demands an answer from the man who caused it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the aggravations of the sin, marks what makes the peril so grievous. David "was a king, whom God had entrusted with the sword of justice and the execution of the law upon other criminals, particularly upon adulterers, who were, by the law, to be put to death; for him therefore to be guilty of those crimes himself was to make himself a pattern, when he should have been a terror, to evil doers" (Romans 2:22). The very Law that condemned Bathsheba's news condemned the king who received it the more deeply. There is no debate among so agreed a company; the verse is a hinge, and they read it as such. Bathsheba's two words do to David what the whole discourse of duty could not: they force the sin out of the dark, and every step he now takes to hide it flows from this moment. What David means to conceal, God means at last to bring to light.
6:
The grammatical-historical and reformed voices open the plot. John Gill locates Joab at the siege of Rabbah, "sixty-four miles from Jerusalem." Keil and Delitzsch supply the mechanics: David has Uriah fetched "upon some pretext," and on his arrival asks "how it fared with Joab and the people and the war" — "probably the pretext under which David had had him sent." Uriah, Keil notes from the roll of heroes (2 Sam. 23:39), was one of David's mighty men; the guess of Josephus that he was Joab's armour-bearer is "without historical foundation." David bids him "go down to thy house, and wash thy feet," and sends a royal present after him.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lays bare the design: David's aim is to "father the child... upon Uriah himself." Henry reads the danger David fears — the injured husband might prosecute his wife to death, for "jealousy is the rage of a man" (Prov. 6:34), or, unable to reach the king by law, might raise a rebellion. Bathsheba's very message, Henry thinks, was an appeal for protection, and "hope of impunity is a great encouragement to iniquity." Charles Ellicott states the scheme in a line: by calling Uriah home and "treating him with marked consideration," David "thought... to have it supposed that the child, begotten in adultery, was Uriah's own."

The witnesses are of one mind: the second sin is subtler than the first and colder. What began as the surprise of the eye has become a calculated management of appearances, and the very courtesies David extends — the summons, the kind inquiry, the dish from the king's table — are all bent to a lie. Henry's warning is the note they land on: "the beginnings of sin are therefore to be dreaded; for who knows where they will end?" The camp at Rabbah and the loyal soldier on the road home are now being arranged around a secret, and the arranging will only drive David deeper. Keil observes that the king's opening question — how Joab, the people, and the war fared — was itself part of the disguise, a show of royal concern designed to make the summons look natural; and the witnesses note the pathos that David, who had abandoned the field, now feigns the anxious commander to the very officer whose life he is preparing to spend. Every courtesy is a calculation, and the calculation reaches, though David does not yet see it, toward the grave.
7:
Only two witnesses linger here, and both read the verse as stagecraft. The reformed John Gill notes that Uriah "came first, before he went to his own house, desirous of knowing what was the special business of the king with him" — the summons had been urgent enough to bring a soldier to the palace before his own door. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch see through the questions David asks — "how Joab did, and how the people did, and how the war prospered" — to their emptiness: this military interest is "probably the pretext under which David had had him sent," a screen of official concern thrown over a private design.

The witnesses let the irony speak. David, who should himself have been in the field putting these questions in person, now plays the anxious commander to the very man he means to wrong. The inquiry after "the war" is made by the one who abandoned it; the solicitude for "the people" comes from the man plotting the death of one of them. Gill and Keil, sober historians both, do not moralize the point at length, but the reader feels it: every honest word in David's mouth has become an instrument of concealment.

There is no dispute in so brief a company, and no need of one. The verse is a bridge — the loyal officer stands before his king, told nothing, suspecting nothing, having crossed the miles of road to answer a summons that hides a snare. What David wants is not the report but the man's night at home; the questions are merely the decent covering under which he steers Uriah toward his own door and, he hopes, toward the concealment of the king's sin. The very brevity of the exchange heightens its treachery, for the fewer the words, the plainer it is that they serve a purpose other than the one they wear. Gill's small observation — that Uriah came to the palace "desirous of knowing what was the special business of the king with him" — sharpens the irony further: the soldier is eager to learn the king's true errand, the one thing David will take pains never to disclose. The witnesses leave the verse as a study in the loneliness of the guilty man, who must now speak past everyone he addresses, meaning always something he cannot say.
8:
David repeats the errand plainly: "Go down to thy house, and wash thy feet," and sends "a mess of meat" after him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies the gift precisely — the word means "a present," the same used of Joseph's portions to his brothers (Genesis 43:34), "some choice dish sent by the king to the guest whom he wished to honour." The reformed John Gill takes the washing of the feet as refreshment "to prepare for bed, which was what he wanted to get him to."

The wesleyan-arminian voices expose the design under the kindness. Adam Clarke: Uriah had come off a journey and "needed this refreshment; but David's design was that he should go and lie with his wife, that the child now conceived should pass for his, the honour of Bath-sheba be screened, and his own crime concealed." Clarke adds the crucial observation about how far David had, and had not, yet gone: "At this time he had no design of the murder of Uriah, nor of taking Bath-sheba to wife" — the whole scheme was still only concealment; "all this was artfully contrived." John Wesley says it in a breath: David sent him down "not doubting but he would there converse with his wife, and so cover their sin and shame."

The witnesses agree that the verse shows sin's cunning before it shows its cruelty. Every gesture — the leave to go home, the gift of food, the royal favour — is calculated, and calculated for a lie. Clarke's note that murder was not yet in David's heart is the sober point they leave with the reader: the king means, at this stage, only to hide what he has done. That the plan will fail, and failing will tempt him to something far worse, is the tragedy the next verses unfold; here he is still a man who thinks a good supper and a night at home will bury his sin. Ellicott's identification of the "present" as a mark of honour lends the scene its final irony: the dish that in Joseph's day signalled a brother's favour (Gen. 43:34) is here the bribe of a guilty king to an unsuspecting subject, and the very language of courtesy is bent to the ends of a lie. The witnesses let the small domestic detail — leave to go home, food sent after — carry the whole moral weight, and turn to watch whether Uriah will walk into the snare.
9:
The plan founders on Uriah's own conduct: he "slept at the door of the king's house with all the servants of his lord, and went not down to his house." The reformed John Gill pictures him falling into conversation with the bodyguard, "well known" to them, then, "weary, laid himself down among them, and slept." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott places the spot exactly — "probably in the guard chamber at the entrance of the palace" (1 Kings 14:27) — and Adam Clarke notes the Eastern custom, that servants "generally sleep on the verandahs or porches in face of their master's house."

Here the witnesses raise, for the first time, the question that will recur: did Uriah know? Ellicott thinks not, and warns against reading suspicion in: "It is quite unnecessary to suppose that Uriah had any suspicion of what had been done. His conduct and language is simply that of a brave, frank, generous-hearted soldier." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the same restraint as heaven's hand — Uriah lay at the door "by the secret direction of God's wise providence, who would bring David's sin to light."

On that last point the company is united, and it is the pastoral weight of the verse. Whether Uriah abstained from suspicion or from soldierly principle, the effect is the same: the very thing David counted on — a husband's natural haste to his own house and wife — is quietly withheld, and the concealment collapses. What David arranges, providence overrules. The soldier's plain integrity, asking nothing and suspecting nothing, becomes in God's hand the instrument that will not let the king's sin be buried, and drives the whole matter on toward the light it was meant to escape. Gill's picture of Uriah lingering in talk with the guard, then lying down among them weary, gives the scene its unstudied realism: there is no defiance in it, only a soldier's habit and a soldier's fatigue, and yet that ordinary faithfulness undoes a king's design. The witnesses, watching the whole train of concealment stall on so slight a thing, read the verse as a quiet parable of how little God needs to overturn the plans of the mighty — a tired man's choice of the wrong bed. What David could not command, Uriah's simplicity refuses without knowing it refuses.
10:
Told that Uriah "went not down unto his house," David presses him the next morning: "Camest thou not from thy journey? why then didst thou not go down?" The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the force of the question — men generally go home "when they return from a journey," so David feigns surprise at the omission. Adam Clarke fills in David's ostensible kindness: "It is not thy duty to keep watch or guard; thou art come from a journey, and needest rest and refreshment." John Wesley voices what David pretends to mean: "Wearied with hard service and travel, nor did I expect... that thou shouldest now attend upon my person, or keep the watch."

The witnesses read the exchange as one more turn of the screw of concealment. David's solicitude is real on its surface and false at its root; he urges the comforts of home upon Uriah precisely to accomplish his own hidden end. The reformed John Gill notes soberly that the report came "either those that went with the mess of meat, or the guards with whom he slept all night" — the palace is watching Uriah, and the king is managing him.

There is no debate among these four; they agree the verse shows David still working the plan, now openly puzzled and pressing where the night before he had only hinted. The pathos is that Uriah's refusal, which David treats as an oddity to be corrected, is in truth a nobility the king can no longer match. The next verse will let the soldier speak for himself, and his answer will shame the very question David has just put to him. For now the king can only wonder aloud at a self-denial he has forgotten how to practise. There is a bitter comedy the witnesses do not miss in David's "why then didst thou not go down?": the king urges upon Uriah the very indulgence — ease, home, the company of his wife — that his own indulgence has already turned into a snare, and he must feign puzzlement at a virtue he was counting on the soldier not to have. Keil's plain note that men naturally go home from a journey only sharpens it, for Uriah's refusal of the natural is precisely the providential hitch on which the whole cover-up begins to break, as the following confession will make plain.
11:
The grammatical-historical commentators honour the answer. Uriah's refusal — "The ark, and Israel, and Judah, abide in tents; and my lord Joab, and the servants of my lord, are encamped in the open fields; shall I then go into mine house, to eat and to drink, and to lie with my wife?" — draws the admiration of every witness. Keil and Delitzsch paraphrase the logic: while "Jehovah with the ark, and all Israel" are camped in the open field in the service of God's kingdom, "it did not become him" to take his ease at home. Charles Ellicott notes the ark was still carried to war as "a symbol of God's presence and pledge of His favour," despite its capture in Eli's day (1 Sam. 4:11), and observes that the separate naming of Israel and Judah is no mark of a late date.

The wesleyan-arminian and reformed voices press the contrast with David. Adam Clarke calls it "the answer of a brave, generous and disinterested man" — "I will not indulge myself while all my fellow soldiers are exposed to hardships, and even the ark of the Lord in danger" — and cannot help adding the question the scene forces: "Had Uriah no suspicion of what had been done in his absence?" John Wesley draws the principle Uriah lived by: when "God's people are in a doubtful and dangerous condition, it becomes me to sympathize with them, and to abstain even from lawful delights." John Gill reads it as Uriah's own apology for a conduct so unlike the king's.

Here the witnesses divide only over Uriah's inward state — whether his self-denial sprang from suspicion (Clarke's recurring doubt) or from sheer soldierly and godly principle (Keil, Ellicott, Wesley) — but they are wholly agreed on its effect. Ellicott states it: "This noble answer of Uriah should have stung David to the quick, but his conscience was so deadened by his sin that the only effect was to lead him to yet baser means of concealment." The pastoral point they land on is stark: the lawful pleasures Uriah refuses for the army's sake are the very pleasures David has stolen unlawfully, and the soldier's restraint is the mirror in which the king ought to have seen himself.
12:
The verse is a single sentence in David's mouth — "Tarry here to day also, and to morrow I will let thee depart" — and only the reformed John Gill glosses it directly, noting that the king detained Uriah "in his court, when he found he could not persuade him to go to his own house." The first night's plan had failed; rather than send the soldier back to the siege, David holds him a second day in Jerusalem, unwilling to let go of a scheme that is slipping from his hands.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, reading the whole train of the plot, sees in this delay the mark of a sin that will not be abandoned. "So Uriah abode in Jerusalem that day, and the morrow," and every hour of that abiding is an hour David spends contriving how to make an honest man serve a dishonest end. Henry's governing lesson presses on the verse: the beginnings of sin "are much to be dreaded; for who knows where they will end?" What began at a rooftop glance has become a days-long management of another man's movements, and the delay is only gathering strength for the baser attempt to come.

There is almost nothing here for the witnesses to dispute, and their near-silence is itself eloquent. The narrator lingers on the tactical detail — a day gained, a departure postponed — precisely to show how much labour concealment costs, and how little it profits. Uriah, unsuspecting, waits at the king's pleasure; David, sleepless in purpose, waits for a chance that the soldier's plain constancy keeps denying him. The verse sets the stage for the next device, when persuasion having failed, David will reach for wine, and the reader already senses that a sin unwilling to stop will not stop short of blood. Gill's single detail — that David kept Uriah in his court "when he found he could not persuade him to go to his own house" — is enough to show the king's mounting frustration: what he cannot obtain by favour he will not yet abandon, and so he buys time. The witnesses, with little to add, let the pause do its work; it is the silence before a worse resolve, and its very emptiness measures how far David has travelled from the man who once trusted God rather than his own contrivance. Concealment, once begun, has no natural stopping-place but the one the next verses will find.
13:
David's second device is drink: "when David had called him, he did eat and drink before him; and he made him drunk." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke states the aim without disguise — David hoped that "in this state he would have been off his guard, and hastened down to his house." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks how the plan failed even so: "Uriah's resolve was so strong that it still governed his conduct while in this almost irresponsible condition"; drunk, he "went out to lie on his bed with the servants of his lord, but went not down to his house." The reformed John Gill introduces the scene as David's invitation of Uriah to sup with him.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the expedient into a moral. To make a man drunk "upon any design whatsoever" is "a very wicked thing," and Henry pronounces over it the prophet's woe against him that gives his neighbour drink (Hab. 2:15) — "God will put a cup of trembling into the hands of those who put into the hands of others the cup of drunkenness." Robbing a man of his reason, Henry says, "is worse than robbing him of his money, and drawing him into sin worse than drawing him into any trouble whatsoever."

The witnesses agree that this is the last rung of the ladder of concealment before it breaks. David has tried courtesy, kindness, and now wine, and each has been foiled by the sober integrity of the man he is trying to corrupt. The very steadfastness that makes Uriah proof against the trap is what seals his fate, for when concealment fails, David — as the morning of the next verse will show — turns from hiding the sin to removing the man. Henry's warning stands over the whole scene: one wickedness must be "covered and secured with another," and there is no telling where the covering will end. Ellicott's phrase for Uriah's condition — that his resolve "still governed his conduct while in this almost irresponsible condition" — is the marvel of the verse: drunkenness, which unmakes most men, could not unmake his fidelity, and the wine meant to loosen him only proved him. The witnesses see in that unlooked-for constancy both the honour of the soldier and the doom of the plan, for a man who will not fail his post even senseless with drink is a man the king cannot manage and, at last, will not suffer to live.
14:
With the morning David crosses from concealment to blood: "David wrote a letter to Joab, and sent it by the hand of Uriah." The grammatical-historical commentators feel the horror of the means. Charles Ellicott: "the brave soldier is made the bearer of his own death-warrant, and his well-known valour for his king is to be the means of accomplishing his destruction." Keil and Delitzsch trace the descent: "when the king saw that his plan was frustrated through Uriah's obstinacy, he resolved upon a fresh and still greater crime," and they pause on its theology — "how deep a soul may fall when it turns away from God... The greatest saints would be ready for the worst of deeds, if Thou shouldst but leave them." The reformed John Gill lays the prompting at the door of the tempter: "Satan put it into his head and heart."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry catalogues the aggravations. The murder was deliberate — "he took time to consider of it," even time to countermand it, "yet he persisted." It was barbarous in its instrument — "he sent the letter by Uriah himself... to make him accessory to his own death." It abused the man's own courage, drew Joab and others into the guilt, and would not let Uriah "die alone," for "some of the people, even the servants of David, fell with him." Henry marvels at the change: "Is this the man whose heart smote him because he had cut off Saul's skirt? Quantum mutatus ab illo! — But ah, how changed!" See how "fleshly lusts war against the soul," blinding the eye and searing the conscience (Prov. 6:32).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the verse's one true outlier — the classical parallel of Bellerophon, sent by Proetus to king Jobates bearing "letters which contained their own condemnation," whence the proverb "to carry one's own condemnation." Yet Clarke, weighing it, finds little real correspondence beyond that single circumstance, and refuses to make the Scripture a copy of the fable. Beneath the differing angles — Keil's meditation on the fall of the saint, Henry's tally of aggravations, Clarke's learned comparison — the witnesses are one: the darkest deed of David's life is done in cold ink, by a trusted hand, against the most loyal of men.
15:
The grammatical-historical commentators read the fatal order. The letter's words are plain murder: "Set ye Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire ye from him, that he may be smitten, and die." Keil and Delitzsch note the king's wish was "fully carried out," Joab stationing Uriah "just where he knew that there were brave men." But Charles Ellicott fastens on the one clause Joab did not obey — "Retire ye from him. This part of David's orders was not carried out." Perhaps Joab "thought it would make the stratagem too evident, or perhaps it was impracticable"; either way "the consequence was that others were slain with Uriah, and thus a larger blood-guiltiness fell upon David."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley voices the moral outrage the order provokes, in words that echo the puritan note: "So far is David from repenting, that he seeks to cover one sin with another. How are the beginnings of sin to be dreaded! For who knows where it will end? David hath sinned, therefore Uriah must die! That innocent, valiant, gallant man, who was ready to die for his prince's honour, must die by his prince's hand." Wesley sees in it the ravage of unchecked lust — "how they blind the eyes, sear the conscience, harden the heart, and destroy all sense of honour and justice." The reformed John Gill simply reads out the orders David gave to Joab.

The witnesses divide only on a point of execution — whether Joab followed the letter to the syllable — and even that division deepens rather than lightens the guilt: by softening the one clause, Joab spared David the transparency of the crime only at the cost of more lives. What unites the company is the recognition that here the sin has reached its full stature. The man who once spared Saul now writes a soldier's death; the concealment that began with a summons ends in a battle deliberately mismanaged, and the innocent, as Ellicott and Wesley alike insist, do not fall alone. Keil marks the cold economy of the order — "that he may be smitten, and die" — in which a soldier's death is engineered with the same care David once gave to sparing life; and the witnesses hear in Wesley's cry, "David hath sinned, therefore Uriah must die," the whole monstrous logic laid bare, that the guilt of the powerful is paid for with the blood of the faithful. It is the point at which, all the company agree, mere concealment has become murder, and the king's letter has made the battlefield the instrument of his private crime.
16:
The verse reports Joab's compliance in a single stroke — "when Joab observed the city, he assigned Uriah unto a place where he knew that valiant men were" — and the two witnesses who linger read it as the general's cold precision. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses the verb: it "means watched, or blockaded"; in the operations of the siege Joab "so arranged some of his forces as to invite a sally from the city under circumstances in which it would be successful." The reformed John Gill takes "observed the city" of Joab's reconnoitring "where lay its greatest strength, and where it was best defended."

The chilling professionalism of it is not lost on them. Joab does not merely place Uriah in danger; he studies the wall, marks where the defenders are bravest, and posts the man precisely there, arranging the very sally that will kill him. Ellicott adds, from the later report, that Uriah's party had been sent "so near as to come within reach of the archers on the wall" (v.24) — the placement designed to draw the enemy out and expose the king's chosen victim.

There is no debate in so short a company, and the verse's brevity is part of its terror. What David planned in ink, Joab performs in the field, without a recorded question or scruple. The general's willingness to sacrifice a valiant officer at a word from the palace is itself a comment on the moral atmosphere the king's sin has created; the machinery of a whole army bends, quietly and efficiently, to hide one man's guilt, and the trap is laid exactly where courage will be sure to answer the challenge. Gill's plainer gloss — that Joab marked "where lay its greatest strength, and where it was best defended" — supplies the tactical detail Ellicott's word confirms: the general chose not a weak point but the strongest, precisely because it would draw the bravest defenders out, and there he set the man he was told to lose. The witnesses let the silence of Joab stand as its own indictment. He asks no reason, records no protest; a valiant officer is posted to die, and the siege of Rabbah goes on around the private murder folded into it, so that the king's sin borrows the whole apparatus of a national war to accomplish and conceal itself.
17:
The sally comes as Joab intended: "the men of the city went out, and fought with Joab: and there fell some of the people of the servants of David; and Uriah the Hittite died also." The reformed John Gill notes the defenders "made a sally out, as Joab expected." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke describes Uriah's end with sober admiration — "he was led to the attack of a place defended by valiant men; and in the heat of the assault, Joab and his men retired from this brave soldier, who cheerfully gave up his life for his king and his country."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch guard against over-reading Joab's part. "The literal fulfilment of the king's command does not warrant us in assuming that Joab suspected how the matter stood, or had heard a rumour concerning it"; rather, "as a general, who was not accustomed to spare human life, he would be a faithful servant of his lord in this point, in order that his own interests might be served another time." The murder is thus done not by a fellow-conspirator who knew the secret but by a hard soldier content to obey a costly order and store up the king's obligation to himself.

The witnesses land together on the pity of it. Uriah dies as he had lived, at his post and for his king, ignorant that the king he served had contrived his fall; and he does not die alone — "some of the people, even the servants of David," go down with him, so that David's single sin spends other innocent lives to hide itself. The brave officer's cheerful courage, which Clarke cannot praise without grief, is the very thing that carries him into the trap; and the sword David called upon in his letter has now done its work. Keil's refusal to blacken Joab beyond the evidence is worth marking: the general need not have known the secret to be its willing instrument, for a hard commander who spends lives freely will obey a costly order and reckon the king thereby in his debt. So the crime is done at two removes — a letter from a guilty king, a shrug from a callous captain — and Uriah, standing between them, pays with his life for a sin not his own. The witnesses close on the grief the narrator himself scarcely voices: that the most loyal man in the army has been killed by the king he was defending, and killed in a manner that spread the loss to others who never knew why they died.
18:
With Uriah dead, Joab turns at once to the palace: "then Joab sent, and told David all the things concerning the war." The two witnesses read the message as a piece of careful diplomacy. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out Joab's instructions to the courier in full — he was to report "all the things of the war... to the end," and then, should "the anger of the king" be kindled at the loss of men so near the wall, to close with the words "Thy servant Uriah the Hethite has perished." The reformed John Gill simply marks the promptness: Joab "sent messengers to David, as soon as Uriah was killed."

The witnesses see in Joab's forethought a man who understands his master. Joab anticipates that David "might possibly be angry... at the fact that Joab had sacrificed a number of warriors by imprudently approaching close to the wall," and prepares the one piece of news that will turn away that anger. The general knows, without being told, that the death of Uriah is the message David most wants to hear, and he arranges his report so that the king's displeasure will dissolve the moment he hears it.

There is no dispute between Gill and Keil; together they expose the grim understanding that has grown up between king and captain. Joab does not name the secret, and perhaps does not know it, but he reads David's interest exactly, and shapes his despatch to soothe a wrath he expects to be feigned. The verse quietly implicates the whole chain of command in David's sin: a soldier is dead, more with him, and the general's chief concern is how the news will sit with the king. Keil's reconstruction of the courier's brief is itself revealing — Joab foresees an angry question about the reckless approach to the wall and prepares its single sufficient answer in advance, which is the death of Uriah. The witnesses observe that Joab thus treats the fall of a hero not as a loss to be mourned but as a card to be played, the one report that will convert the king's displeasure into secret content. Gill's note that Joab sent "as soon as Uriah was killed" adds the last cold touch: there is no pause, no decent interval, only the swift despatch of a man who knows exactly what his master has been waiting to hear.
19:
The verse continues Joab's briefing of the courier: "when thou hast made an end of telling the matters of the war unto the king..." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the whole shape of the instruction — the messenger is to relate the events "to the end," and only if the king's wrath rises at the army's rashness in coming so near the wall is he to play his final card. The reformed John Gill notes that Joab "gave him a particular direction... what he should say at the close of his narrative, according as he should observe the king's countenance."

The witnesses dwell on the psychology. Joab has rehearsed the courier in reading David's face: the report is to be measured out and its climax withheld until the king's mood requires it. Keil observes that the messenger, in the event, "so far condensed it as to mention Uriah's death at the same time" — the human bearer would abbreviate the general's careful script. The scene shows Joab treating the death of a hero as the concluding move in a courtier's game of anticipated anger and calculated relief.

The two witnesses do not differ, and their agreement is itself the point. The verse is a study in how sin corrupts the ordinary channels of an army: a battlefield despatch is composed not to inform but to manage a guilty king, and a brave man's name is reserved as the sweetener at its end. Joab, whether or not he knows the secret, has grasped that Uriah's death is the news that will make all the rest forgivable, and he coaches his messenger accordingly, watching the king's countenance for the cue. Gill's phrase — that the direction was "according as he should observe the king's countenance" — turns the courier into a reader of faces, instructed to hold or release his last item by the flicker of royal displeasure. The witnesses find in this the chapter's grimmest small irony: an army's despatch, which ought to render a plain account of the living and the dead, is choreographed instead to soothe a king whose composure is itself a lie. Even the honest bearer of the news is drawn, unknowing, into the management of David's guilt, so that the sin spreads outward through every hand it touches.
20:
The verse gives the words Joab puts in the king's mouth by anticipation: "if so be that the king's wrath arise, and he say unto thee, Wherefore approached ye so nigh unto the city?" The reformed John Gill notes the wrath "might be seen in his countenance, or expressed in his words." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke offers a shrewd conjecture about Joab's own stake in the matter: "it is likely that Joab had by some indiscretion suffered loss about this time; and he contrived to get rid of the odium by connecting the transaction with the death of Uriah, which he knew would be so pleasing to the king."

On Clarke's reading the verse serves two men at once. Joab foresees that the costly approach to the wall may draw a rebuke; but he also knows the one announcement that will convert royal anger into royal satisfaction. By binding the report of his own tactical loss to the news of Uriah's fall, the general shields himself under the very death he was ordered to arrange. The wrath David is expected to feel over spilt blood will be quenched, the witnesses see, by the report of the blood David most desired to be spilt.

Gill and Clarke stand together in reading the verse as one more turn of concealment, now practised by the captain as deftly as by the king. The anticipated question about Abimelech and the wall is not really about tactics at all; it is the frame in which Uriah's death can be slipped in as its answer. What should be a soldier's honest accounting of a hard day has become, in Joab's hands, a managed performance, calculated to the temper of a guilty master. Clarke's conjecture, that Joab may have "by some indiscretion suffered loss about this time" and seized on Uriah's death to bury his own fault, only widens the circle of self-interest: king and captain alike find their advantage in the same corpse. The witnesses do not press the guess beyond its warrant, but they let it stand as a plausible reading of a scene in which everyone has something to hide behind Uriah's fall. Gill's plainer note about the king's wrath, whether shown in look or word, keeps the focus where the verse sets it — on a message shaped less to inform than to appease.
21:
The rehearsed speech reaches its historical example: "Who smote Abimelech the son of Jerubbesheth? did not a woman cast a piece of a millstone upon him from the wall, that he died in Thebez?" The reformed John Gill unfolds the name — Jerubbesheth is Jerubbaal, that is Gideon, "Baal" being softened to "Bosheth," which "signifies shame," a shameful idol; the son smitten at Thebez was Abimelech (Judg. 9:53). The allusion is to a famous death beneath a city wall, exactly the folly of which David might accuse his general.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the courtier's craft in it. Joab "anticipated David's anger at his apparent rashness, and charged the messenger, when he should observe it, to mention Uriah's death." The stroke was well judged: it "was not likely to awaken any suspicion in the messenger, as it would appear... rather as an effort on Joab's part to throw the blame from himself upon Uriah as the leader of the assaulting party." In the event, Ellicott notes, "the messenger appears to have told all in one breath, so that there was no opportunity for David to express displeasure."

Ellicott adds the historical aside the whole company would relish — "the reference to the case of Abimelech shows how familiar the Israelites were with the past history of their people" — that a common courier could be trusted to carry a precedent out of the book of Judges. The witnesses do not divide; together they show a whole machinery of memory and message bent to a single end, the smoothing of a king's conscience. The ancient warning against fighting too near a wall is invoked not for its wisdom but as the cue for the name that will make all well: "Thy servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also." Gill's unfolding of the shameful name — Jerubbaal turned to Jerubbesheth, the honoured judge Gideon shadowed by the idol Baal softened into "shame" — lends the allusion an unintended aptness, for a story of shame is being used to cover a deed of shame. The witnesses observe that the whole rhetorical machinery Joab builds is aimed at a wrath that will never come; the precedent, the anticipated rebuke, the reserved announcement, all presuppose a grieving king, and the next verses will show there is none. So the craft is wasted on its object even as it succeeds, and the elaborate speech only exposes how well captain and king already understand each other.
22:
The verse is barely a sentence — "So the messenger went, and came and shewed David all that Joab had sent him for" — and only two witnesses pause over it. The reformed John Gill notes simply that the man went "from Joab, from the army before Rabbah." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe what the messenger did with his instructions: he "brought to David all the information with which Joab had charged him," but "so far condensed it as to mention Uriah's death at the same time," compressing the careful, staged report into a single telling.

The witnesses read a small but telling human touch here. Joab had rehearsed a two-stage message — the account of the battle first, and the name of Uriah held back until the king's anger should call it forth — but the courier, either from haste or from a soldier's instinct, poured out the whole at once. The general's calculated choreography of royal wrath and courtly relief is overtaken by the plain manner of the man who actually delivered it.

There is nothing here for Gill and Keil to dispute; the verse is a hinge between Joab's design and David's response. Yet even in its brevity it carries the chapter's irony forward: the elaborate management of the news scarcely matters, for the king who receives it is not angry at all. The messenger's blunt, all-at-once report will meet not the feigned wrath Joab feared but the cold composure of a man who has got exactly what he wanted, as the next verses reveal. Keil's single observation — that the courier "so far condensed it as to mention Uriah's death at the same time" — carries a quiet human comedy: the general had scripted a careful crescendo, and the plain soldier delivered it as one flat sentence, indifferent to the courtly art of timing. The witnesses find the collapse of Joab's design instructive. All the calculation about the king's mood proves needless, for the king needs no managing; the very over-elaboration of the message is the measure of how little those around David suspect the truth, and how much David himself has hidden even from his most trusted captain.
23:
The courier's own account fills the verse: "Surely the men prevailed against us, and came out unto us into the field, and we were upon them even unto the entering of the gate." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the fuller report that runs on into the next verse — how "the men of Rabbah became strong against us, and came out to us into the field, and we prevailed against them even to the gate," and then "the archers shot" from the wall. The reformed John Gill introduces it as "the particulars of his account," the plain narrative of a sally repulsed to the city gate.

The witnesses note the shape of the messenger's telling. He casts the day as a near-success — the besiegers had driven the defenders back "unto the entering of the gate" — before coming to the loss under the wall. Keil's rendering shows the tactical picture: the garrison sallied, was pressed hard almost into the city, and then took its toll from archers posted on the ramparts. It is precisely the "coming near the wall" that Joab had foreseen the king might question, now set within a report of momentary advantage.

The two witnesses do not differ; they let the courier's words carry the weight. The battle is described honestly enough as far as it goes, but its whole point, for the man who sent the message and the king who hears it, lies in the single name still to come. The verse holds the reader on the threshold — the assault told, the losses about to be named — so that the death of Uriah, when it lands in the next line, may fall with the force Joab intended it to have. Keil notes that the messenger's telling folds Joab's two-stage plan into one breath, so that the very "coming near the wall" which the general feared would provoke the king is here narrated frankly, without apology, as though the courier trusted the plain truth to carry itself. The witnesses observe that this artless honesty on the battlefield stands in sharp relief against the studied dishonesty in the palace: the soldier reports a hard fight straightforwardly, while the king who hears it has turned the whole engagement into the cover for a murder. The gap between the two is the moral distance the chapter has been measuring all along.
24:
Only the reformed John Gill glosses this verse, and he keeps close to the picture it draws: "the shooters shot from off of the wall upon thy servants," loosing "arrows out of their bows, or stones out of their engines," while "the Israelites following them so closely to the gate of the city came within the reach of their shot from the wall." The very nearness to the wall that Joab had anticipated the king might question is here the immediate cause of the losses; the assaulting party, pressing the sally back to the gate, exposed itself to the archers above.

The verse brings the courier's report to its calculated climax — "some of the king's servants be dead, and thy servant Uriah the Hittite is dead also." The name that Joab had reserved as the answer to royal anger arrives at last, laid quietly at the end of a list of the fallen. Gill's plain reading lets the horror sit in the ordinary language of a battle report: a handful of the king's men are dead, and among them, as if by the mere chance of war, the one man the king had marked for death.

With so single a witness there is no debate, and none is needed. The verse is the hinge on which the whole elaborate scheme turns: the letter written, the placement contrived, the sally drawn, the arrows loosed, and now the report delivered — all of it converging on the flat announcement of Uriah's death. What Joab designed as the message that would appease the king's wrath is spoken; and the next verse will show how little wrath there was to soothe, and how coldly David receives the tidings he had himself commanded. Gill's plain rendering keeps the horror in the register of an ordinary war report: arrows and engine-stones from the wall, servants pressing too close, a handful fallen — and Uriah's name added last, "as if by the mere chance of war," when in truth it was the whole object of the exercise. The witnesses let that disguise stand as the verse's chief lesson. A private murder has been dressed in the impersonal language of siege-craft, so that the one death David sought is buried in a list of the incidental dead; and only the reader, who has seen the letter written, knows that the "chance" was contrived from the first line of David's hand.
25:
David's reply is the coldest speech in the chapter: "Let not this thing displease thee, for the sword devoureth one as well as another: make thy battle more strong against the city, and overthrow it: and encourage thou him." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render it with its studied composure — "David received with apparent composure the intelligence which he was naturally so anxious to hear," and sent Joab word not to "look upon this affair as evil," but to press the siege and "destroy it." The reformed John Gill notes the king "dispatched the messenger again to Joab upon the delivery of his message."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke and the grammatical-historical Ellicott expose the hypocrisy beneath the calm — though they weigh it differently. Clarke is blunt: "What abominable hypocrisy was here! He well knew that Uriah's death was no chance-medley; he was by his own order thrust on the edge of the sword." Charles Ellicott reads the message as more cunning still — "ostensibly to encourage Joab, on the ground that the mishap was a mere accident of war," yet "couched in such language as to imply a special regret for the loss of Uriah." The phrase "one as well as another," Ellicott paraphrases, means: "though Uriah was a brave hero whom we could ill spare, yet in the fortune of war we cannot choose who shall fall."

Here the witnesses differ only in emphasis — Clarke sees naked hypocrisy, Ellicott a subtler feigned regret — but both agree the sentence is a lie dressed as soldierly resignation. The proverb about the impartial sword, true enough on any other lips, is on David's the covering of a murder he himself contrived. And the closing word to Joab — "encourage thou him" — binds the general more closely to the king in a shared secret, so that the message meant to comfort the captain is in truth the seal of their partnership in blood. The composure the witnesses hear is the most damning note of all: David is not grieved but relieved. Keil's word "apparent composure" holds the whole tragedy of the moment, for the calm is real on its surface and hollow beneath: the king who could not bear the death of his enemy Saul now takes the death of his faithful servant as good news, and dresses his satisfaction in a proverb. The witnesses agree that with this reply the sin reaches its coldest point — not the heat of lust nor the panic of concealment, but the settled, comfortable acceptance of a murder accomplished, spoken in the tone of a man dismissing an unlucky day.
26:
The verse records the widow's mourning: "when the wife of Uriah heard that Uriah her husband was dead, she mourned for her husband." The reformed John Gill supposes "the news of which were soon sent her by David," who "chose to have his death published abroad as soon as possible, the more to hide his sin." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott asks how long the mourning lasted — "the usual period was seven days" (Genesis 50:10; 1 Sam. 31:13) — and though a widow's grief "may well have been somewhat longer, it was doubtless, under the circumstances, made as short as was consistent with decency." Keil and Delitzsch mark the plain fact of her lament.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke doubts the depth of the sorrow. "The whole of her conduct," he judges, "indicates that she observed the form without feeling the power of sorrow. She lost a captain and got a king for her spouse; this must have been deep affliction indeed" — and he seals the irony with the poet's line about tears not falling of their own accord and groans forced out "from a joyful heart." Here the witnesses divide: where Clarke reads a hollow ceremony, the plainer narrators let the mourning stand as at least the decent observance the Law and custom required.

Yet even the division is narrow, for all sense the unreality of the scene. A woman mourns a husband whose death her paramour arranged and hastened to announce; the rites of grief are kept precisely so that the marriage they clear the way for may look decent. The witnesses let the seven brief days speak for themselves — a covering as thin as the composure of the previous verse — and turn, with the last line of the chapter already in view, toward the verdict that no mourning and no marriage can avert. Gill's observation that David published Uriah's death "as soon as possible, the more to hide his sin" exposes the calculation even in the funeral: the widow's grief is set on its brief course precisely so that the marriage may follow with the least indecent haste. The witnesses see the whole scene, then, as one more arrangement — the last of the chapter's many managed appearances — in which real feeling, if there is any, is subordinate to the demands of concealment. And they leave the reader with the sense that all this contrivance is about to meet the one Spectator it cannot manage, whose displeasure the closing verse will name.
27:
The chapter ends where sin always ends, before God: "when the mourning was past, David sent and fetched her to his house, and she became his wife, and bare him a son. But the thing that David had done displeased the LORD." The grammatical-historical witnesses mark the haste. John Gill notes David "stayed not ninety days from the death of her husband," the later Jewish rule meant to establish a child's paternity, and records that "because David did not wait this time, Abarbinel charges it upon him as an additional sin." Charles Ellicott observes that "several months must have passed since the beginning of David's course of sin," and yet "his conscience had not brought him to a sense of what he had done, nor had the prophet Nathan been sent."

The wesleyan-arminian voices weigh the marriage and the impenitence. Adam Clarke sees the wedding as the last device of concealment — "this hurried marriage was no doubt intended... to cover the pregnancy" — and reads the final clause as Scripture's necessary guard: it was added "lest the splendour of David's former virtues should induce any to suppose his crimes were passed over, or looked on with an indulgent eye, by the God of purity and justice." John Wesley draws the sobering doctrine from the long delay: that "David continued in the state of impenitency for divers months together, and this notwithstanding his frequent attendance upon God's ordinances" — an eminent instance of "the corruption of man's nature, of the deceitfulness of sin, and of the tremendous judgment of God in punishing one sin by delivering a man up to another."

Clarke's closing meditation is the chapter's great outlier, a long peroration in which he refuses every attempt to varnish the story. That Scripture records so black a fall in one of "its brightest ornaments" he takes as proof of its honesty — no forger "would have inserted such an account" — and he apportions the blame with almost epigrammatic severity: "I pity David; I venerate Uriah; I detest Joab, and think meanly of Bath-sheba." Beneath the differing angles the witnesses are wholly one on the last clause, which Ellicott and Wesley alike make the door to what follows: God has seen, God is displeased, and though the king has silenced every human accuser, the divine verdict stands, and the prophet is already on his way (2 Sam. 12:1).
